 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a81.7","title":"E2E: Stripe Connector Compliance Run","description":"# E2E: Stripe Connector Compliance Run

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-a8xp","title":"TEST: Checkpoint/Frontier Unit Tests (Advancement, Fork Detection, Convergence)","description":"# TEST: Checkpoint/Frontier Unit Tests


## Goal
Validate ZoneCheckpoint/frontier advancement and fork detection semantics with deterministic unit/integration tests.

## Scope
Comprehensive tests for checkpoint management and fork detection.

## Test Categories

### Checkpoint Advancement
- [ ] Normal advancement with quorum signatures
- [ ] Advancement blocked without quorum
- [ ] Checkpoint sequence monotonically increases
- [ ] Checkpoint binds AuditHead + RevocationHead
- [ ] Checkpoint signature verification

### Fork Detection
- [ ] Conflicting checkpoints detected
- [ ] Fork evidence is preserved
- [ ] Fork triggers alert/pause
- [ ] Fork resolution protocol
- [ ] No silent fork acceptance

### Convergence
- [ ] Nodes converge on same checkpoint
- [ ] Late-joining node catches up
- [ ] Partition recovery reaches consistency
- [ ] Checkpoint gossip propagation
- [ ] Convergence metrics tracking

### Frontier Management
- [ ] Frontier tracks latest valid checkpoint per zone
- [ ] Stale frontier detection
- [ ] Frontier update atomic with heads

## Golden Vectors
Location: `tests/vectors/checkpoint/`
- `checkpoint_valid.cbor`
- `checkpoint_fork_evidence.cbor`
- `convergence_scenario.json`

## Logging Requirements
```json
{
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test_name": "test_fork_detection",
  "phase": "execute",
  "correlation_id": "uuid",
  "zone_id": "z:work",
  "checkpoint_seq": 42,
  "fork_detected": true,
  "result": "pass"
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fork detection is reliable
- Convergence is achieved after partition heal
- All checkpoint operations are audit-logged

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-abwt","title":"TEST: Canonical Serialization Unit Tests (Deterministic CBOR, Schema Hash, CDDL Validation)","description":"# TEST: Canonical Serialization Unit Tests

## Goal
Prove that serialization is deterministic and schema-bound.

## Test Categories

### Deterministic CBOR Encoding
- [ ] Same logical object → same bytes (always)
- [ ] Map key ordering is canonical (sorted)
- [ ] Integer encoding is minimal
- [ ] Float encoding is canonical
- [ ] No duplicate map keys
- [ ] Definite length encoding used

### Schema Hash Binding
- [ ] SchemaId (namespace, name, version) hashes correctly
- [ ] SchemaHash is 32 bytes (BLAKE3)
- [ ] Payload format: schema_hash || canonical_cbor_bytes
- [ ] Schema hash mismatch detected on decode

### Schema Validation
- [ ] Valid object passes schema check
- [ ] Missing required field rejected
- [ ] Wrong field type rejected
- [ ] Extra fields handled per policy (reject or ignore)
- [ ] Nested object validation works

### CDDL Compliance
- [ ] All FCP2 objects have CDDL definitions
- [ ] Generated bytes match CDDL constraints
- [ ] CDDL can be used for cross-language validation

### Roundtrip Tests
- [ ] Encode → decode → encode produces identical bytes
- [ ] All core object types roundtrip correctly
- [ ] Large objects roundtrip correctly
- [ ] Nested objects roundtrip correctly

### Bounds and Limits
- [ ] Oversized objects rejected at encode time
- [ ] Deeply nested objects bounded
- [ ] String length limits enforced
- [ ] Array length limits enforced

### Decode Safety
- [ ] Malformed CBOR rejected without panic
- [ ] Truncated input rejected
- [ ] Oversized length prefix rejected (DoS prevention)
- [ ] Recursive structure depth bounded

## Golden Vectors
- Each core object type with canonical bytes
- Schema hash computation examples
- Malformed inputs that must be rejected

## Property Tests
- Determinism: same input → same output
- Commutativity with schema: schema_hash(decode(encode(x))) == schema_hash(x)

## Fuzz Targets
- CBOR decode with arbitrary bytes
- Schema validation with malformed objects

## Logging Requirements
```json
{
  "test_name": "...",
  "object_type": "...",
  "schema_id": "...",
  "operation": "encode|decode|validate",
  "result": "pass|fail",
  "reason_code": "..."
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All core types have golden vectors
- Determinism verified by property tests
- Fuzz targets find no panics

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-agh5","title":"TEST: fcp-ratelimit Unit Test Suite","description":"# TEST: fcp-ratelimit Unit Test Suite

## Scope
Comprehensive unit tests for the rate limiting library.

## Test Categories

### Token Bucket Algorithm
- [ ] Basic request allowance/denial
- [ ] Bucket refill timing
- [ ] Burst handling
- [ ] Multiple buckets per endpoint

### Sliding Window
- [ ] Window rollover behavior
- [ ] Request counting accuracy
- [ ] Boundary conditions

### Rate Limit Header Parsing
- [ ] X-RateLimit-Limit parsing
- [ ] X-RateLimit-Remaining parsing
- [ ] X-RateLimit-Reset parsing
- [ ] Retry-After header handling
- [ ] Various header formats (GitHub, Twitter, Stripe, etc.)

### Backoff Strategies
- [ ] Exponential backoff
- [ ] Jitter calculation
- [ ] Maximum retry limits
- [ ] Backoff reset conditions

### Concurrency
- [ ] Thread-safe bucket access
- [ ] Atomic counter operations
- [ ] Race condition prevention

### Quota Management
- [ ] Daily/hourly quota tracking
- [ ] Quota reset timing
- [ ] Multiple quota types (read vs write)

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-aho","title":"fcp.s3: AWS S3 Compatible Object Storage Connector","description":"# fcp.s3 - AWS S3 Compatible Object Storage Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide an FCP2-compliant S3-compatible object storage connector (AWS S3 and compatible endpoints) for bucket/object operations with strict NetworkConstraints and safe credential injection.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Strategic Context

### Value Assessment
**Strategic Value Score: 81/100** — Important Tier 3 connector (Infrastructure/Storage family).

**Why Critical**: S3 is the **de facto standard for object storage**, used by virtually every cloud application. S3-compatible APIs are offered by AWS, GCP, MinIO, Cloudflare R2, etc. Essential for agents to read/write files, manage assets, and handle data storage.

### Priority Matrix Position
- **Category**: Strategic Investment (Infrastructure + Universal)
- **Build Phase**: Phase 2 Productivity (weeks 5-8)

---

## Agent Capabilities (Concrete Use Cases)

### Object Operations
- Upload/download objects
- List objects in buckets
- Delete objects
- Generate presigned URLs

### Metadata Management
- Read object metadata
- Set custom metadata
- Content type handling
- Cache control

### Bucket Operations
- List accessible buckets
- Get bucket configuration
- Check permissions

### Examples of Agent Workflows
```
"Upload this file to S3"
"Download the latest backup"
"List all files in the assets bucket"
"Generate a shareable link for this file"
"Archive old logs to cold storage"
"Sync this folder to S3"
"What's in the exports bucket?"
```

---

## Flywheel Ecosystem Synergies

### ALL File-Based Connectors
**Universal file storage:**
- Store exports from any connector
- Central asset repository
- Backup destination

### Google Drive/OneDrive Integration
- S3 as backend storage
- Cross-cloud file sync
- Enterprise file management

### AI Provider Integration
- Store model artifacts
- Dataset management
- Training data access

### Terraform/Kubernetes Integration
- Infrastructure state storage
- Container image registry backing
- Configuration storage

### Agent Mail Integration
- Large attachment storage
- File sharing links
- Document archival

---

## Technical Implementation Notes

### Provider Compatibility
S3-compatible APIs:
| Provider | Endpoint |
|----------|----------|
| AWS S3 | s3.{region}.amazonaws.com |
| MinIO | Custom endpoint |
| Cloudflare R2 | {account}.r2.cloudflarestorage.com |
| GCS (interop) | storage.googleapis.com |
| DigitalOcean Spaces | {region}.digitaloceanspaces.com |

### Recommended Crates
- `aws-sdk-s3` — Official AWS SDK
- `rusoto_s3` — Alternative SDK
- `reqwest` — For custom implementations

### Authentication
- AWS Signature V4
- IAM credentials
- STS temporary credentials
- Service account keys

### Storage Classes
| Class | Use Case |
|-------|----------|
| STANDARD | Frequently accessed |
| INTELLIGENT_TIERING | Variable access |
| GLACIER | Archive |
| DEEP_ARCHIVE | Long-term archive |

---

## Zone Architecture

### Recommended Zones
| Zone | Description | Capabilities |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| `z:storage:s3:full` | Full access | All operations |
| `z:storage:s3:readonly` | Read-only | Download and list |
| `z:storage:s3:bucket:<name>` | Bucket-scoped | Single bucket |
| `z:storage:s3:write` | Write-only | Upload only |

### Data Sensitivity
- S3 often contains sensitive data
- Bucket policies critical
- Encryption requirements vary

---

## Manifest Archetypes (Closed Set)
- `operational` — REST API operations

---

## External Surface

### NetworkConstraints
Varies by provider:
- AWS: `s3.*.amazonaws.com:443`
- Custom: Configured endpoint

### Credential Injection
- AWS credentials via `CredentialId`
- Access key ID + Secret
- STS tokens for temporary access
- Never write credentials to disk

---

## Capability Model

### Read Operations
- `s3.objects.read` — Download objects
- `s3.objects.list` — List objects
- `s3.objects.metadata` — Get metadata
- `s3.buckets.list` — List buckets

### Write Operations
- `s3.objects.write` — Upload objects
- `s3.objects.copy` — Copy objects

### Administrative (Dangerous)
- `s3.objects.delete` — Delete objects
- `s3.buckets.manage` — Bucket operations

### Presigned URLs
- `s3.presigned.get` — Generate download URLs
- `s3.presigned.put` — Generate upload URLs

---

## Operations (Representative)

### List Objects
```rust
let resp = client.list_objects_v2()
    .bucket("my-bucket")
    .prefix("exports/")
    .max_keys(100)
    .send()
    .await?;
```

### Upload Object
```rust
client.put_object()
    .bucket("my-bucket")
    .key("path/to/file.txt")
    .body(ByteStream::from_path("local/file.txt").await?)
    .content_type("text/plain")
    .send()
    .await?;
```

### Download Object
```rust
let resp = client.get_object()
    .bucket("my-bucket")
    .key("path/to/file.txt")
    .send()
    .await?;
let body = resp.body.collect().await?;
```

### Generate Presigned URL
```rust
let presigned = client.get_object()
    .bucket("my-bucket")
    .key("path/to/file.txt")
    .presigned(PresigningConfig::expires_in(Duration::from_secs(3600))?)
    .await?;
```

### Delete Object
```rust
client.delete_object()
    .bucket("my-bucket")
    .key("path/to/file.txt")
    .send()
    .await?;
```

---

## Multi-Provider Support

### Configuration Pattern
```rust
struct S3Config {
    provider: S3Provider,  // AWS, MinIO, R2, etc.
    endpoint: Option<String>,
    region: String,
    bucket: String,
    path_style: bool,  // true for MinIO
}
```

### Provider-Specific Notes
- **MinIO**: Requires path-style URLs
- **R2**: No bucket creation API
- **GCS**: Limited S3 compatibility
- **Spaces**: Full S3 compatibility

---

## Tests

### Unit Tests (Mock-Only)
- Request signing
- Response parsing
- URL generation
- Provider configuration

### Integration Tests
- LocalStack or MinIO for simulation
- Multi-part upload testing
- Large file handling

### E2E Compliance
- Validates NetworkConstraints
- Bucket-level access control
- Audit logging for all operations

---

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- No real S3 calls in unit tests
- Upload/download works
- List operations functional
- Presigned URLs work
- Multi-provider support
- Large file handling (multipart)

## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-k4ka","title":"fcp-graphql: GraphQL Client Infrastructure Library","description":"# fcp-graphql: GraphQL Client Infrastructure Library


## Goal
Provide a shared, typed GraphQL client library for connectors with retries/pagination/subscriptions, mapping errors to the FCP taxonomy and fully mock-tested.

## Overview
Shared GraphQL client library for connectors that interface with GraphQL APIs (Linear, GitHub, Notion, Shopify, etc.).

## Features

### Core Client
- Async GraphQL client with connection pooling
- Query/mutation/subscription support
- Automatic retry with exponential backoff
- Request batching and deduplication
- Query complexity estimation

### Query Building
- Type-safe query builder
- Fragment composition
- Variable handling
- Directive support

### Pagination
- Cursor-based pagination (Relay-style)
- Offset-based pagination
- Automatic page traversal
- Total count estimation

### Subscriptions
- WebSocket transport
- Reconnection handling
- Subscription multiplexing
- Heartbeat management

### Introspection
- Schema introspection caching
- Type validation
- Deprecation warnings

### Error Handling
- GraphQL error parsing
- Partial data handling
- Network error recovery
- Rate limit detection

## Dependencies
- reqwest (HTTP)
- tokio-tungstenite (WebSocket)
- serde_json
- fcp-core

## Consumers
- fcp.linear (GraphQL-only API)
- fcp.github (GraphQL API v4)
- fcp.notion (REST+GraphQL hybrid)
- fcp.shopify (Admin API)

## Testing
- Mock GraphQL server
- Response recording/playback
- Schema validation tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- The functionality described in this bead is implemented with typed schemas + validation (no unstructured “stringly” interfaces).
- Errors map to the FCP error taxonomy with correct retry semantics and no secret/PII leakage.
- Mock-only unit/integration tests cover success paths and common failures and emit structured JSON logs per `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-k57","title":"fcp.jira: Bidirectional Beads sync integration","description":"# fcp.jira: Bidirectional Beads sync integration (FCP2)

## Goal
Implement **bidirectional synchronization** between Flywheel Beads issues and Jira issues for the connector’s bound zone.

This enables:
- Jira → Beads: convert incoming Jira work into native Beads (agent-ready tasks)
- Beads → Jira: propagate Beads updates back into Jira to keep humans in their existing workflows

## Non-goals
- “Backwards compatibility” with any older sync format or FCP1 behavior.
- Cross-zone sync from a single connector instance (still single-zone bound).

## Core requirements (hard)
- **Deterministic mapping** (same inputs produce same object updates).
- **Loop prevention** (Jira update triggers Beads update triggers Jira update… must stop).
- **Conflict detection** (concurrent edits) with explicit policy:
  - default: fail-closed + raise a conflict event
  - optional: last-write-wins only if explicitly enabled
- **Idempotency**: repeated webhook deliveries or retries must not duplicate issues/comments.

## Scope
### Mapping surface (minimum viable)
- Bead fields ↔ Jira fields:
  - title ↔ summary
  - description ↔ description (ADF/markdown conversion)
  - status ↔ workflow status
  - priority ↔ priority mapping
  - labels ↔ labels
  - assignee ↔ assignee
  - due date ↔ duedate
  - estimates ↔ timeoriginalestimate (if policy allows)
  - bead_id ↔ a dedicated custom field (or label) for stable correlation

### Sync directions
- `jira_to_beads`
- `beads_to_jira`
- `bidirectional`

### Event sources
- Jira webhooks (issue created/updated/commented/transitioned).
- Beads change events (local event stream from the platform).

## Connector state model (FCP2)
This bead MUST use the platform connector-state mechanism:
- Store a persistent mapping table:
  - `bead_id ↔ jira_issue_id/key`
  - last_seen_revision / fingerprint
  - last_sync_origin (jira|beads)
  - tombstones for deletions/archives
- Use singleton-writer lease semantics if the connector runs in multiple locations.

## Capability + approval model
Illustrative capabilities:
- `jira.issues.read` / `jira.issues.write`
- `jira.comments.write`
- `jira.webhooks.manage` (dangerous; provisioning only)

Policy guidance:
- Creating/transitioning issues in Jira is a write and must produce receipts/audit.
- Bulk sync operations may require an ApprovalToken depending on org policy.

## Deliverables
- A sync engine with:
  - field mapping layer
  - ADF ↔ markdown conversion
  - conflict detection + resolution policy
  - loop prevention via origin stamps + revision fingerprints
- Operation schemas:
  - `jira.sync.push_bead`
  - `jira.sync.pull_issue`
  - `jira.sync.reconcile`
- Structured logging:
  - correlation_id per sync run
  - per-item reason codes for skips/conflicts

## Test strategy (MUST)
### Unit/integration (mock-only)
- Webhook replay tests:
  - duplicate webhook deliveries do not duplicate Beads/Jira updates
- Conflict tests:
  - concurrent edits produce conflict events and do not silently overwrite
- Loop tests:
  - Beads update → Jira update → webhook does not re-trigger Beads update
- State tests:
  - mapping table persists across restarts
  - lease enforcement prevents double writers

### E2E compliance
- Use the shared compliance runner to confirm:
  - default deny on all sync operations
  - receipts/audit for all writes
  - NetworkConstraints enforcement

## Acceptance criteria
- A round-trip sync of a single issue converges in ≤2 sync cycles without loops.
- Conflict cases are explicit, logged, and surfaced as events.
- Sync behavior is deterministic and idempotent.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-kjk","title":"fcp.jira: Issue CRUD with custom fields support","description":"# fcp.jira: Issue CRUD with Custom Fields Support

## Goal
Implement the core Jira issue lifecycle operations with robust custom-field handling.

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Scope
Operations (representative):
- `jira.issue.create`
- `jira.issue.get`
- `jira.issue.update`
- `jira.issue.delete` (Dangerous)
- `jira.issue.assign`
- `jira.issue.link` / `jira.issue.unlink`

## Key requirements
- Capability gated (read vs write vs delete).
- Custom fields:
  - discover field IDs and contexts
  - handle typed values correctly (selects, multi-select, cascading, user, sprint/epic link)
  - avoid relying on field display names (unstable)
- Text format:
  - Jira Cloud uses ADF; implement deterministic markdown ↔ ADF conversions
- Idempotency / receipts:
  - writes emit OperationReceipt + AuditEvents
  - delete is Dangerous and should require ApprovalToken `Execution` scope

## Tests
- Mock-only tests cover:
  - field discovery + context differences
  - ADF conversion round-trip
  - permission failures and rate limits

## Acceptance Criteria
- Issue CRUD works across Cloud and Server/DC with explicit feature gating.
- Custom fields are handled deterministically and safely.

5. **must_use_candidate**: Added `#[must_use]` to `with_missing_hint()`
6. **return_self_not_must_use**: Covered by above `#[must_use]`
7. **must_use_candidate**: Added `#[must_use]` to `has_proof_of_need()`
8. **unnecessary_map_or**: Changed `.map_or(false, ...)` to `.is_some_and(...)`

## Files Changed

- `crates/fcp-protocol/src/fcps.rs`

## Verification

- `cargo clippy --package fcp-protocol -- -D warnings` passes
- All 72 fcp-protocol tests pass","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T01:36:40.360788866-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T01:36:53.3287167-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-17T01:36:53.3287167-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","labels":["clippy","fcp-protocol"]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-krt","title":"fcp-telemetry: Unified Metrics, Logging, and Tracing Library","description":"# fcp-telemetry: Unified Metrics, Logging, and Tracing Library

## Overview
Comprehensive observability library providing structured logging, metrics collection, and distributed tracing for FCP connectors.

## Structured Logging
- JSON output format
- Log level filtering (trace → error)
- Automatic field injection:
  - Correlation ID
  - Zone ID
  - Connector ID
  - Request ID
- Sensitive data redaction
- Async log writing

## Metrics Collection
- Counter metrics (requests, errors)
- Gauge metrics (connections, queue depth)
- Histogram metrics (latencies, sizes)
- Timer helpers
- Label/tag support
- Pre-defined FCP metrics

## Distributed Tracing
- Span creation and nesting
- Context propagation (W3C Trace Context)
- Trace ID generation
- Span attributes
- Sampling strategies
- Cross-connector correlation

## Export Formats
- Prometheus exposition format
- OTLP (OpenTelemetry Protocol)
- JSON logs to stdout
- Custom sinks

## Integration
- tracing crate integration
- HTTP middleware for auto-instrumentation
- FCP operation decoration
- Health endpoint exposure

## Performance
- Low overhead (<1% CPU)
- Async export
- Batch flushing
- Sampling support

## Dependencies
- tracing, tracing-subscriber
- opentelemetry, opentelemetry-otlp
- metrics, fcp-core","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-11T10:37:41.061916979-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T12:47:47.279327696-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T12:47:47.279327696-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented fcp-telemetry crate with structured logging, metrics, distributed tracing, Prometheus and OTLP export"}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-kt9r","title":"[FCP2] Credential Injection: SecretObject + CredentialObject + Secretless Egress (Zeroize)","description":"# [FCP2] Credential Injection: SecretObject + CredentialObject + Secretless Egress (Zeroize)

## Goal
Define how credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens, webhook secrets, database passwords, client certs) flow to connectors in a way that is:
- mechanically capability-gated
- single-zone bound
- audited and explainable
- secret-safe (no disk; no logs; prefer no plaintext in connector memory)

## Why this is MVP
Connectors cannot do useful work without credentials, and credentials are one of the highest-value compromise targets. If we get this wrong, every connector becomes an exfiltration vector.

This bead defines the *platform contract* for credential access, so connector authors can implement auth once and rely on mechanical enforcement.

## NORMATIVE object model (Spec + `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md`)

### 1) `SecretObject` + `SecretAccessToken`
- Secrets are stored/represented as mesh objects (`SecretObject`).
- Using/reconstructing a secret requires an explicit, short-lived authorization (`SecretAccessToken`).
- Every successful access MUST emit an `AuditEvent` (e.g., `event_type = "secret.access"`) and MUST NOT include secret bytes.
- Secret material MUST be zeroized immediately after use.

Note: Threshold secrets (k-of-n) and wrapped shares are part of the spec security model. Even if we ship a simplified MVP path, the *interfaces* must not preclude threshold reconstruction.

### 2) `CredentialObject` + `CredentialId` ("secretless" by default)
A `CredentialObject` is a zone-bound, auditable handle describing *how to apply* a secret to outbound traffic:
- maps `CredentialId` -> `SecretId`
- defines application method (e.g., HTTP header format)
- optionally binds allowed hosts for defense-in-depth

Connectors SHOULD NOT receive raw credential bytes. They should reference a `CredentialId` in egress requests and rely on MeshNode enforcement.

### 3) Capability constraints: `credential_allow`
Capabilities grant *permission to use credentials* by listing allowed `CredentialId`s:
- `CapabilityConstraints.credential_allow` is the authoritative allow-list.
- Credential permissions are checked at the boundary (egress proxy / host), not by connector convention.

## Preferred flow: secretless credential injection via MeshNode egress proxy
1. Connector prepares an `EgressHttpRequest` (or `EgressTcpConnectRequest`) and sets `credential = Some(CredentialId)`.
2. Egress proxy verifies ALL of the following:
   - `CredentialId` ∈ invoking capability’s `credential_allow`
   - destination host/port conforms to `NetworkConstraints`
   - destination host is allowed by the referenced `CredentialObject.host_allow` (when present)
   - policy allows the operation’s safety tier in this zone (fail closed)
3. Proxy fetches/validates the referenced `CredentialObject` and `SecretObject` and injects credential material at the network boundary.
4. Proxy emits an `AuditEvent` describing usage (credential id, host, purpose) without any secret bytes.

This yields "secretless connectors" where plaintext credentials never enter connector memory.

## When secret materialization is unavoidable (explicit, audited, bounded)
Some connectors may need to sign payloads locally (HMAC), perform mTLS client auth, or interact with non-proxied libraries.

In these cases:
- The connector (or host on its behalf) must obtain a `SecretAccessToken`.
- Reconstruction/use must be short-lived and bounded.
- Every materialization MUST be audited.
- Dangerous operations MUST require explicit ApprovalToken policy (and emit DecisionReceipt on deny).
- Secret bytes MUST be zeroized on all paths, including errors and panics.

## Manifest / schema integration requirements
- Credential IDs MUST NOT be encoded inside capability IDs.
- Operations that need credentials must require a capability whose constraints include the needed `CredentialId` in `credential_allow`.
- If credentials are host-bound, host canonicalization rules MUST match the NetworkConstraints canonicalization rules.

## Tests (owned by `flywheel_connectors-nnja`)
- Unit: `CredentialObject` validation (format, host binding, canonicalization)
- Unit: `credential_allow` enforcement logic (allow/deny)
- Integration: egress proxy injection (header injection, deny wrong host, deny missing `credential_allow`)
- Adversarial: prove connectors cannot obtain secret bytes unless explicitly granted and audited
- Audit: `secret.access` events emitted with redaction guarantees

## Acceptance Criteria
- Secretless credential injection works end-to-end with mechanical enforcement at the boundary.
- No secrets touch disk; no secret bytes appear in logs or error messages.
- Unauthorized credential use fails closed with a DecisionReceipt and evidence.
- Secret materialization (when required) is explicit, audited, short-lived, and zeroized.
","notes":"Core types implemented: SecretObject, SecretId, SecretAccessToken, SecretMaterial (with zeroize), CredentialObject, CredentialId, CredentialApplication variants, hex_or_bytes_vec serde helper. Added credential_allow to CapabilityConstraints. All tests pass. Commit a8d6286. Remaining: egress proxy integration (depends on 1n78.18).","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T02:53:07.542098129-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T22:47:58.262851142-05:00","labels":["fcp2","mvp","secrets","security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-kt9r","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T02:54:36.462831896-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-kt9r","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T02:54:36.560080415-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-kt9r","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1n78.18","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-15T02:54:36.633658392-05:00","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-l25","title":"fcp.terraform: Resource import for existing infrastructure","description":"# fcp.terraform: Resource import for existing infrastructure (FCP2)

## Goal
Allow controlled `terraform import` so agents can bring existing infrastructure under state management.

Import mutates state and can be risky; require approval gating.

## Scope
- Import a resource into state with explicit address + provider id.
- Validate inputs and prevent injection into CLI arguments.
- Emit receipts/audit for the import action.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `terraform.import` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- Sandbox profile restricts filesystem + process execution.
- NetworkConstraints default deny unless provider APIs are explicitly allowed.
- Approval gating recommended.

## Deliverables
- Operation schema:
  - `terraform.import`
- Input validation:
  - strict address grammar
  - strict provider id format

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Unit tests with a terraform shim for:
  - arg construction
  - approval gating
  - audit/receipt emission

## Acceptance criteria
- Imports are mechanically authorized, safe, and auditable.
